heroTip: Thailand is a mixed-payment destination — carry Thai Baht cash for street food and local shops, and use a low-fee card for hotels and malls.
bestCard: Choose a card with zero foreign transaction fees — Wise, Revolut, or a travel credit card. Always pay in THB to avoid DCC charges.
topTips: Street food stalls, local markets, and tuk-tuks are almost always cash-only — keep THB on you.
Shopping malls, chain restaurants, and hotels widely accept Visa and Mastercard.
Most ATMs in Thailand charge a ฿220 fee for foreign cards — withdraw larger amounts less often to minimise this.
PromptPay QR is popular for local-to-local transfers but requires a Thai bank account.
Avoid airport exchange counters on arrival — rates are worse; use a city ATM or exchange booth instead.
Always choose to pay in Thai Baht (THB) at the terminal — never accept DCC.
If spending over ฿2,000 in eligible stores, ask about the 7% VAT refund available to tourists.
avoidTip: Avoid currency exchange at Suvarnabhumi or Don Mueang airports — rates are typically 3–5% worse than in the city.
dccNote: At many tourist-area shops and restaurants in Thailand, card terminals will offer to charge you in your home currency. Always select Thai Baht — the merchant's DCC rate is typically 3–10% above the real market rate.

fxFeeItems.name: FX Fee (Foreign Transaction Fee)
fxFeeItems.detail: Fee your bank charges for overseas card use — typically 1.5%–2.5% of the transaction amount.

fxFeeItems.name: Spread
fxFeeItems.detail: The gap between the mid-market rate and the rate your bank actually gives you — usually 0.5%–2%.

fxFeeItems.name: ATM Fee
fxFeeItems.detail: Thai ATM operators charge a flat ฿220 fee per foreign-card withdrawal on top of any fee from your home bank.

fxFeeItems.name: DCC Markup
fxFeeItems.detail: Extra markup when you pay in your home currency — often 3%–10% above the true exchange rate.
